examples.title: Fetch Cycle Microoperations
examples.description: Demonstrates the microoperations performed during the fetch phase of instruction cycle
examples.explanation: The fetch cycle retrieves an instruction from memory into the CPU. PC holds the address of the next instruction, which is transferred to MAR. Memory is read, instruction is stored in MBR, then transferred to IR. Finally, PC is incremented to point to the next instruction.

examples.title: Simulating Instruction Execution
examples.description: Python program to simulate basic instruction fetch-decode-execute cycle with CPU registers
examples.code:
class CPU:
    def __init__(self):
        self.PC = 0       # Program Counter
        self.IR = None    # Instruction Register
        self.ACC = 0      # Accumulator
        self.MAR = 0      # Memory Address Register
        self.MBR = None   # Memory Buffer Register
        self.memory = [None] * 256  # Simple memory
    
    def fetch(self):
        """Fetch instruction from memory"""
        self.MAR = self.PC
        self.MBR = self.memory[self.MAR]
        self.IR = self.MBR
        self.PC += 1
        print(f"Fetched: {self.IR} from address {self.MAR}")
    
    def decode_execute(self):
        """Decode and execute instruction"""
        instr = self.IR
        if instr is None:
            return
        
        opcode = instr[0]
        
        if opcode == 'LOAD':
            self.ACC = instr[1]
            print(f"LOAD: ACC = {self.ACC}")
        elif opcode == 'ADD':
            self.ACC += instr[1]
            print(f"ADD: ACC = {self.ACC}")
        elif opcode == 'STORE':
            addr = instr[1]
            self.memory[addr] = self.ACC
            print(f"STORE: M[{addr}] = {self.ACC}")
        elif opcode == 'HALT':
            print("Program halted")

# Example program
cpu = CPU()
cpu.memory[0] = ('LOAD', 10)
cpu.memory[1] = ('ADD', 5)
cpu.memory[2] = ('STORE', 100)
cpu.memory[3] = ('HALT',)

print("=== Execution ===")
while cpu.memory[cpu.PC] and cpu.memory[cpu.PC][0] != 'HALT':
    cpu.fetch()
    cpu.decode_execute()
print(f"Final ACC: {cpu.ACC}, Memory[100]: {cpu.memory[100]}")
examples.explanation: This Python simulation demonstrates how a CPU executes instructions. It implements the fetch-decode-execute cycle using a class with CPU registers (PC, IR, MAR, MBR, ACC). The fetch method retrieves instructions from memory, decode_execute processes LOAD, ADD, STORE, and HALT opcodes.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of instructions

examples.title: Instruction Cycle Simulation in C
examples.description: C program demonstrating the instruction cycle phases with CPU registers
examples.explanation: This C program simulates the complete instruction cycle. It demonstrates: FETCH (PC→MAR→memory→MBR→IR, PC+1), DECODE (extract opcode and address from instruction), and EXECUTE (perform operation based on opcode). Uses a simple instruction format where upper 8 bits are address and lower 8 bits are opcode.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of instructions executed
examples.spaceComplexity: O(256) for memory array + O(1) for registers

examples.title: CPU Instruction Cycle in Java
examples.description: Java class simulating CPU with fetch-decode-execute cycle demonstrating instruction processing
examples.explanation: This Java program provides a complete object-oriented simulation of CPU instruction cycle. It demonstrates FETCH (retrieve instruction), DECODE (extract opcode and address), and EXECUTE (perform LOAD/ADD/STORE operations). The instruction format uses 4-digit numbers: first digit=opcode, last 3=digits=address.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of instructions
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) plus O(256) for memory array
